name: Mammon
description: Mammon, within the Abrahamic traditions, is a complex figure often personifying greed, materialism, and the relentless pursuit of wealth. Unlike other deities with established cults or mythologies, Mammon appears primarily as a symbolic representation of the corrupting influence of earthly riches. While not explicitly named as a demon in early biblical texts, later interpretations, particularly in medieval demonology, associate him with the infernal hierarchy. This association stems from scriptural warnings against serving both God and Mammon, emphasizing the inherent conflict between spiritual devotion and the allure of material possessions. Mammon represents the seductive power of wealth, capable of leading individuals astray from righteousness and fostering avarice. His enduring presence in literature and art serves as a cautionary figure, embodying the dangers of unchecked ambition and the ethical compromises often made in the pursuit of financial gain. The concept of Mammon thus functions as a critical commentary on societal values and the potential for wealth to eclipse moral considerations.
subtitle: Lord of Greed

keyMyths.title: Serving God and Mammon
keyMyths.description: The central myth associated with Mammon is not a narrative, but a dictum found in the Gospels: 'No one can serve two masters. Either you will hate the one and love the other, or you will be devoted to the one and despise the other. You cannot serve both God and Mammon.' This statement highlights the fundamental conflict between spiritual values and the pursuit of wealth, suggesting that devotion to material gain inherently detracts from religious piety. It establishes Mammon as an antithetical force to divine worship.

keyMyths.title: Mammon in Paradise Lost
keyMyths.description: John Milton's 'Paradise Lost' offers a vivid depiction of Mammon as one of the fallen angels. In this epic poem, Mammon is portrayed as a creature who admires the riches of Hell, preferring them to the glories of Heaven. He advocates for remaining in Hell and exploiting its resources, embodying a pragmatic and materialistic approach to their fallen state. This portrayal solidified Mammon's image as a figure associated with earthly wealth and a rejection of spiritual aspirations.
keyMyths.source: Milton, John. Paradise Lost. Book I.

keyMyths.title: Mammon as a Demon
keyMyths.description: In later demonological traditions, Mammon is classified as a demon associated with greed and avarice. He is believed to tempt individuals with the promise of wealth and power, leading them down a path of moral compromise and corruption. This interpretation draws upon the earlier warnings against serving Mammon and expands upon it by personifying him as a malevolent entity actively seeking to undermine faith and virtue through the allure of material possessions. This demonization further reinforced the negative connotations surrounding wealth and its potential for moral decay.
